truth.papers.A73.title: Fe–Mn–C–Al Low-Density Steel for Structural Materials: A Review of Alloying, Heat Treatment, Microstructure, and Mechanical Properties
truth.papers.A73.paper_scope: The article reviews recent progress in alloying and heat treatment design and their effects on microstructural evolution and mechanical properties of Al-containing Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steel, with emphasis on work hardening mechanisms, tribological performance, cryogenic behavior, and hydrogen embrittlement.

prediction.papers.A73.title: Fe–Mn–C–Al Low-Density Steel for Structural Materials: A Review of Alloying, Heat Treatment, Microstructure, and Mechanical Properties
prediction.papers.A73.paper_scope: Review of alloying and heat treatment effects on microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steels, covering work hardening mechanisms, tribological behavior, cryogenic toughness, and hydrogen embrittlement.

truth.alloys.alloy_fully_austenite.alloys_note: Fully austenitic low-density steel with high Mn, Al, and C contents to stabilize austenite at room temperature; characterized by annealing twins and fully recovered grains.
truth.alloys.alloy_austenite_duplex.alloys_not: Austenite-based duplex steel consisting of austenite and ferrite mixture; ferrite often elongated along the rolling direction.
truth.alloys.alloy_fe28mn8p5al1c1p2si.alloys_n: Used to study austenite decomposition and κ-carbide precipitation during cooling and aging (Figure 3).
truth.alloys.alloy_fe22p33mn1p35c9p87al.alloys: Used for wear behavior study under different aging temperatures (500–600°C).
truth.alloys.alloy_fe28mn1c8p5al.alloys_notes: Investigated for wear behavior after quenching and aging; overaging leads to coarse κ and microcracking.
truth.alloys.alloy_fe22mn0p4c1p5al1p5si.alloys: Processed by metal injection molding (MIM) and sintered at 1360°C; contains manganese oxide particles in austenitic matrix.

prediction.alloys.alloy_fe22mn06c6al.alloys_notes: 6 wt% Al addition eliminates the TWIP effect and yields approximately 9% density reduction.
prediction.alloys.alloy_fexmn045c2al.alloys_notes: Mn content varied at 17, 19, and 22 wt% for tensile and Charpy impact studies.
prediction.alloys.alloy_fe18mn06cxal.alloys_notes: Al content varied at 3, 5, and 8 wt% for cryogenic impact energy study.

Annealing treatment to obtain fully austenitic microstructure with annealing twins.

truth.processes.proc_fe28mn8p5_cool_control.de: Controlled cooling from austenitization to influence κ precipitation; slow cooling below 4.4°C/h leads to κ formation.
truth.processes.proc_fe22p33_aging.description: Homogenization followed by aging at 500°C, 550°C, and 600°C for 1–3 hours.
truth.processes.proc_fe28mn1c8p5al_q_t.descrip: Quenching from 1050°C followed by aging at 550°C for 4 h or at 350°C for 3 h.
truth.processes.proc_fe22mn0p4_sinter.descript: Metal injection molding (MIM) process with sintering at 1360°C for 1 h or 3 h.

prediction.processes.proc_fe22p33_aging.processes_n: Investigation of wear rate as a function of aging time and temperature; incoherent κ particles form within austenite matrix.
prediction.processes.proc_fe28mn1c8p5al_q_t.descrip: 1050 °C quenching and subsequent aging at 550 °C for 4 h.
prediction.processes.proc_fe28mn1c8p5al_q_t.process: Overaging treatment causing κ particle coarsening around grain boundaries and wear deterioration.

truth.characterization_methods.sample_fully_au: Observe microstructure of fully austenitic low-density steel
Abundant annealing twins observed; grains are fully recovered.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_duplex_a: Observe duplex microstructure of austenite-based low-density steel
Elongated ferrite phase grains along the rolling direction observed within austenite matrix.

Observe microbands and coplanar slip bands induced by SRO
Narrow and dense coplanar slip bands prove grain subdivision and dynamic Hall-Petch hardening effect.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_fe22p33_: Observe worn surface and wear debris
600 °C aged steel shows good wear resistance with craters on worn surface.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_fe28mn1c: Observe worn surface and microcrack propagation
Delamination and microcracking observed along broken coarse κ particles in 550 °C overaged sample.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Validated using experimental isothermal sections at 900°C.
truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Estimate density of Fe-Mn-C-Al austenitic steel
C addition has strongest density reduction effect per weight percent.
Calculate austenite lattice constant from composition
C atom (interstitial) expands lattice more than substitutional Al and Mn.
Al and C solutes strengthen by lattice expansion; Mn has little effect.
Predict transition from wavy to planar slip with Al content
Lower W_R indicates stronger planar slip due to SRO; parameter decreases with Al.
Estimate yield strength increment from carbides
Small, high volume fraction carbides (TiC, VC, NbC) provide greater strengthening.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Hardening rate of martensite stronger than ferrite, bainite, and retained austenite; necking strain of martensite 0.5 higher than bainite 0.127, ferrite 0.163, and retained austenite 0.348.
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: yield strength improvement by carbide precipitation
Δσp = 0.847·M·(1.2/π)·G·b·√(3/2π)·f^0.5/D·ln(D/2b); higher volume fraction and smaller size of Ti, V, Nb carbides improve yield strength.
prediction.computational_details.idx_1.validation_a: Isothermal sections of Fe-(10,20,30 wt%)Mn-xC-yAl at 900 °C validated against experiments.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The review emphasizes that Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steel can achieve tensile strength up to 1.1 GPa with ductility ≥40%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Alloying design: C addition reduces density most strongly (4 times that of Al), but solid solubility limit of C in austenite is below 1.2 wt% to avoid crack formation; Al is considered the most suitable alloying element for both density reduction and yield strength improvement.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Phase composition: fully austenitic steels require Mn 12-30 wt%, Al 5-12 wt%, C 0.6-1.2 wt%; austenite-based duplex steels have Mn 5-30 wt%, Al 5-10 wt%, C 0.4-0.7 wt%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Isothermal phase sections at 900°C (Figure 2) show that with increasing Mn, γ region expands in high Al area (>8 wt%), κ phase region expands at high C (>3 wt%) and high Al (>10 wt%); M3C and κ phases appear above 1.2 wt% C; α phase region expands with high Al.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Aging sequence for γ decomposition (valid for Mn 12-30%, Al ≤12%, C 0.6-2.0 wt%): γ → γ' + γ'' → γ' + L1₂ (SRO) → γ + κ. Slow cooling (<4.4°C/h) causes κ precipitation in Fe-28Mn-8.5Al-1C-1.2Si steel.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: κ-carbide precipitates in high Al steels (≥7 wt%); Fe(Mn)₃C dominates in low Al steels (<7 wt%). Overaging coarsens κ particles and reduces work hardening capacity.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: TWIP effect is suppressed by Al addition (6 wt% Al eliminates TWIP in Fe-22Mn-0.6C-6Al steel) due to increased SFE; TWIP is mainly active in low Al steels.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: TRIP effect operates in austenite-based duplex steels with C < 0.5 wt% and Al 3-6 wt%, where retained austenite transforms to martensite under strain, delaying necking.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: SRO effect promotes planar dislocation slip in high Al (≥6 wt%) steels; dislocation substructures (Taylor lattices, cells) contribute to work hardening.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Alloying effects summary (Table 1): Al reduces density but weakens TWIP; Nb improves yield stress but weakens TWIP; C enhances twinning but may cause shear fracture via DSA; Mn improves cryogenic toughness but has little effect at room temperature.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: For Fe-17Mn-0.45C-2Al, Fe-19Mn-0.45C-2Al, and Fe-22Mn-0.45C-2Al steels at cryogenic temperature, ductility decreased to 75.7%, 53.4% and 9.7% of room-temperature values, and Charpy impact energy decreased to 80.5%, 49.8% and 41.1%, respectively. Martensite formed in the 17Mn steel.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_11.value: For Fe-18Mn-0.6C-xAl steels at cryogenic temperature: impact energy was 122 J for 3Al, 138 J for 5Al, but only 18 J for 8Al due to δ-ferrite formation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_12.value: Room-temperature Charpy impact energy of 22Mn steel was 149 J vs 44 J for 18Mn steel; Mn stabilizes austenite and improves low-temperature toughness.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_13.value: Wear behavior: For Fe-22.33Mn-1.35C-9.87Al, aging at 600°C for 3 h produced good wear resistance with incoherent κ inhibiting crack nucleation. For Fe-28Mn-1C-8.5Al, overaging at 550°C for >2 h caused coarse κ along grain boundaries, leading to microcracking and delamination.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_14.value: MIM-processed Fe-22Mn-0.4C-1.5Al-1.5Si steel sintered at 1360°C produced fully austenitic matrix with uniformly distributed manganese oxide particles (Figure 9).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_15.value: Hydrogen embrittlement: Al addition mitigates HE by increasing hydrogen solubility and reducing diffusivity.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_16.value: Production challenges include low heat dissipation of high-Mn ingots, Al-oxide pollution during casting and rolling, and chemical inhomogeneity; solutions such as CaO-SiO₂, CaO-Al₂O₃ mold fluxes and B₂O₃-Li₂O-BaO fluxing agents are being explored.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_17.value: Future directions: optimize aging to balance strength/ductility with κ precipitation; reduce Al and Mn usage to lower cost; develop hot stamping technology for forming accuracy.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: General density range of Fe-Mn-C-Al low-density steel: 6.2-7.0 g cm^-3 with Al addition from 2 to 12 wt%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: General mechanical property ranges reported: yield strength up to 950 MPa, tensile strength up to 1.1 GPa, plastic elongation not less than 40%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: For Fe-xMn-0.45C-2Al steel, Charpy impact energy at room temperature: 149 J for 22 wt% Mn steel and 44 J for 18 wt% Mn steel.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: For Fe-xMn-0.45C-2Al steel at cryogenic temperature, ductility retained 75.7%, 53.4%, and 9.7% of room temperature values for 17, 19, and 22 wt% Mn steels respectively; impact energy retained 80.5%, 49.8%, and 41.1%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: For Fe-18Mn-0.6C-xAl steel at cryogenic temperature, impact energy increased from 122 J (3 wt% Al) to 138 J (5 wt% Al), but dropped to 18 J at 8 wt% Al due to δ-ferrite formation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Wear rate data for Fe-22.33Mn-1.35C-9.87Al: as-homogenized 0.35 mm^3/Nm; after aging at 500 °C for 1-3 h: 0.29, 0.24, 0.21 mm^3/Nm; at 550 °C: 0.24, 0.19, 0.17 mm^3/Nm; at 600 °C: 0.18, 0.14, 0.11 mm^3/Nm.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: TRIP-effect low-density steel can reach 950 MPa tensile strength with optimized cold rolling of 45% reduction.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Necking strains by phase: martensite 0.5, bainite 0.127, ferrite 0.163, retained austenite 0.348 (from ABAQUS/UMAT simulation).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Fe-22Mn-0.6C-6Al steel shows approximately 9% density reduction but TWIP effect is eliminated.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Hydrogen embrittlement sensitivity in Fe-Mn-C-Al steel is mitigated by Al addition through enhanced hydrogen solubility and reduced hydrogen diffusivity.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: Phase decomposition sequence in Fe-Mn-C-Al: γ → γ′ + γ″ → γ′ + L1₂(SRO) → γ + κ, occurring at 400-650 °C for 0.5-100 h within Mn: 12-30 wt%, Al: up to 12 wt%, C: 0.6-2.0 wt% range.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_11.value: κ precipitation occurs in high Al-containing steel (≥7 wt%), while Fe(Mn)₃C carbide is commonly observed in low Al-containing steel (<7 wt%).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_12.value: Challenges for industrial application: low heat dissipation rate of high-Mn ingots and Al-oxide pollution during casting and rolling.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_15.value: Yield strength empirical relations: σγ = 10.5 Al_wt% + 351.5; σγ = 0.58 Mn_wt% + 328.0; σγ = 180.7 C_wt% + 250.7.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_16.value: Dislocation slip mode parameter: W_R = γ/μ - 2(1+ν)b_eΩc/π(1-ν), indicating stronger planar slipping with increasing Al content.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_17.value: Table 1 summary: Al reduces density but weakens TWIP; Nb improves yield stress but weakens TWIP; C strengthens twinning but causes unpredictable shear fracture; Mn improves cryogenic properties but offers little room-temperature benefit.
